It is a great mercy for young people, especially for young men of high status like Jehoash, to have those around them who will instruct them to do what is right in the sight of the Lord; and they act wisely and well for themselves when willing to be advised and guided. The temple was in disrepair; Jehoash ordered its repair. The king was zealous. God requires those who have power to use it to support religion, address injustices, and fix what has fallen into ruin. The king employed the priests to manage the repairs, believing they would be most enthusiastic about the work. But nothing was accomplished effectively until the twenty-third year of his reign. Another approach was therefore taken. When public funds are distributed honestly, people will contribute willingly. While they were gathering all they could for the repair of the temple, they did not reduce the regular support given to the priests. Let not the servants of the temple be left without resources under the pretense of repairing its damage. Those who were entrusted with the task performed it carefully and faithfully. They did not spend money on decorations for the temple until the other work was finished; from this we can learn, in all our expenses, to prioritize what is most necessary, and when dealing with public funds, to act as we would for ourselves.

Let us examine the character of Jehoash and consider what we may learn from it. When we see such a sad ending to so promising a beginning, it should prompt us to investigate our spiritual declines. If we know anything of Christ as the foundation of our faith and hope, let us desire to know nothing but Christ. May the work of the blessed Spirit on our souls be evident; may we see, feel, and earnestly seek after Jesus in all his fullness, suitability, and grace, so that our souls may be brought from dead works to serve the living and true God.
